justification: All pass criteria met: both terminology mismatches (session/token and suspended/locked) explicitly identified and mapped in a dedicated terminology_alignment section with clear origin attribution (Bird vs MJ). Both ACs preserved with exact error codes SESSION_EXPIRED and ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED. Architecture details include Redis TTL 1800, last_accessed_at field, and token issuance step for locked accounts. The brief uses consistent terminology throughout with notes on when to use which term (e.g., 'token' in code, 'session' in user-facing messages; error code stays ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED even though internal field is 'locked').

observations.text: terminology_alignment section is exceptionally well-structured, mapping both pairs with explicit definitions and usage guidance for Shaq
Correctly flags that 'locked' must never leak into external API responses — catches the most likely implementation mistake
Origin attribution is clear: Bird's vocabulary vs MJ's vocabulary explicitly named so Shaq understands the provenance
Both ACs preserve exact error codes SESSION_EXPIRED and ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED verbatim
Architecture guidance includes all required details: Redis TTL 1800, last_accessed_at, token issuance check for locked accounts, middleware pattern
Open questions are thoughtful and practical — atomicity of TTL reset, key schema, and additional account states are all real implementation concerns
The closing notes section reinforces the key risk (leaking 'locked' into external responses) without being redundant

    at the API boundary so callers receive predictable, spec-compliant error
